one_line_takeaway: GUIDE is an open, modular courseware repository (slides+video+Colab+paper per unit) that turns published LLM-for-chip-design research -- including hardware-security attack and defense papers cited as related work -- into standardized, deployable teaching units; the paper itself is an education/curriculum-design contribution, not a new security attack, defense, or evaluation.
key_finding: No empirical research finding is reported. The paper's evidence is deployment/adoption data: GUIDE units have been used across six course offerings at NYU, NYU Abu Dhabi, and RPI, plus a hackathon and a student security competition (CSAW 2025), demonstrating the courseware is reusable in practice across different instructors, student populations, and learning goals (from intro digital design to team-based ASIC tapeout to hardware-security attack/defense capstones).
core_contribution: GUIDE (GenAI Units In Digital Design Education) is an open-source courseware repository (https://github.com/FCHXWH823/LLM4ChipDesign) that packages LLM-aided digital-design research into standardized, reusable 'teaching units.' Each unit follows a fixed structure -- slides, a short video, a runnable Google Colab lab, and a related paper -- and must meet quality bars (runnable from scratch, end-to-end runnable example, evidence artifacts for grading, stable file layout, clear explanations). The repository is organized into three topics: LLM-aided RTL generation, LLM-aided RTL verification, and LLM-aided hardware security (attacks and defenses). The paper's own contribution is this pedagogical architecture and its instantiation, not any new attack, defense, dataset, or model.
threat_model: N/A -- this is not a security research paper. It contains no threat model of its own. It organizes prior hardware-security research (LLMPirate, ATTRITION, GHOST, RTL-Breaker, Security Assertions, NOODLE, NSPG, TrojanLoC, LockForge, SALAD -- refs [18]-[27]) as citable teaching content for a 'hardware attacks' / 'hardware defenses' curriculum subtopic, but does not itself propose, implement, or evaluate an attack or defense.
limitations: The paper does not evaluate learning outcomes (e.g., pre/post assessment, grade distributions, skill transfer) -- it reports enrollment counts and deliverable types, not pedagogical effectiveness. It is a single research group's/collaborators' repository and course set (NYU, NYU Abu Dhabi, RPI, Texas A&M authors), so generalizability to other institutions is not measured. The 'related papers' backing several units (e.g., the hardware-security attack/defense units) are third-party research the authors did not reproduce or independently validate for teaching purposes beyond citing them. The security-relevant units (LLMPirate piracy rewriting, GHOST-based Trojan insertion capstone) are described as classroom/CSAW exercises using published techniques, not as new security contributions -- risk of dual-use is not discussed.

quantitative_results: Build your ASIC I: 12 students enrolled
Build your ASIC II: 6 project teams
GUIDE4HardwareSecurity (RPI, Spring 2026, taught by Dr. Basu): 34 students enrolled, teams of 2-4 for two capstone projects worth 50% of course grade
Hardware Design at NYU-AD (Spring 2026): 10 computer-engineering students
NYU Cognichip Hackathon: 72 students across 24 international teams (21 US, 1 Canada, 2 India)
